text.title: Azure Well-Architected Framework
text.summary: Five pillars of the Azure Well-Architected Framework with concrete implementation guidance per pillar.

rule.statement: Design for failure recovery at every scale: deploy across availability zones, implement circuit breakers and retry with exponential backoff, and plan explicit data recovery paths.
rule.rationale: Azure SLA for single-VM is 99.9%. Zone-redundant deployments achieve 99.99%. The difference is 8 hours vs 52 minutes of downtime per year.

list.item: Use availability zones for critical VMs, databases, and load balancers
Configure geo-redundant storage (GRS/GZRS) for data with RTO < 4h requirements
Implement health checks and auto-healing via VMSS or AKS readiness probes
Define and test recovery procedures for ransomware — Azure Backup immutable vaults

rule.statement: Implement Zero Trust: never trust, always verify. Use Microsoft Entra ID for all identity, Managed Identity for service-to-service auth, and Private Endpoints for PaaS service access.
rule.rationale: Network perimeter security fails when an attacker is already inside the network. Zero Trust authenticates every request regardless of source, reducing lateral movement after initial compromise.

list.item: Replace service principal keys with Managed Identity wherever possible
Use PIM (Privileged Identity Management) for just-in-time admin access
Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud and respond to Secure Score recommendations
Use customer-managed keys (CMK) in Azure Key Vault for regulated data
Private Endpoints for Azure Storage, SQL, Key Vault — no public internet exposure

rule.statement: Right-size before committing: use Azure Monitor metrics to identify P95 CPU/memory usage, then apply Azure Reservations for a 1-3 year commitment at 40-72% discount.
rule.rationale: Reservations without right-sizing commits to overprovisioned resources. Right-sizing alone without reservations pays on-demand prices. Both steps together maximize savings.

list.item: Azure Reservations: up to 72% savings on VMs, 40% on SQL
Azure Savings Plans: up to 65% across compute families
Spot VMs: up to 90% for interruptible workloads (CI, batch, dev)
Auto-shutdown dev VMs on schedule: 50%+ savings on dev environments
Use Azure Cost Management + budgets + alerts to prevent surprise bills

rule.statement: Deploy all infrastructure via IaC (Bicep or Terraform); never make manual changes in the portal for production resources.
rule.rationale: Manual portal changes create configuration drift that is invisible to code reviewers, cannot be rolled back, and is not reproducible for disaster recovery.

list.item: Use Azure Verified Modules (AVM) for new deployments — maintained by Microsoft with security best practices baked in
Automate deployments through CI/CD pipelines with staging environment first
Use Azure Monitor + Log Analytics + Application Insights for unified observability
Define runbooks for all Tier 1 operations — no tribal knowledge

rule.statement: Scale horizontally before vertically — add instances via VMSS or AKS HPA before increasing VM size.
rule.rationale: Vertical scaling requires downtime or live migration. Horizontal scaling is elastic and resilient. Azure VMSS supports autoscale based on CPU, memory, and custom metrics.

list.item: Use Azure Cache for Redis to eliminate repeated database reads for hot data
Azure CDN / Front Door for static assets and global routing
Use Async processing (Service Bus, Event Grid) to decouple heavy operations from request path
Establish performance baselines in staging before production release
